channel.title: Free
channel.generator: Webpage HTML Export plugin for Obsidian

item.title: Agents & Agentic Behavior
item.description:
I have a list of places to get agents and skills.An agent is a runtime controller that wraps your model and gives it structured abilities: Planning : break tasks into steps
Tool use : call functions, MCP servers, APIs Observation loops : read tool output and continue State : keep track of goals, memory, context Autonomy : decide what to do next Agents are not the model, Agents are not the prompt. Agents are the logic layer that turns a model into a worker.Agents unlock capabilities that raw LLMs cannot do: Write code → run code → fix error Search the web → extract data → summarize Read files → modify files → commit to Git Query databases → transform → export Plan multi-step tasks → execute → refine Without agents, your model is just a text generator in a single "GO"...Think of an Agent as a structure , where the AI plans , executes and tests and has a "loop" until it reaches the desired goal These files define the agent’s identity and behavior, including: its role its capabilities
its tools (MCP servers, function calls) its planning strategy
its Memory Usage its workflow rules its safety constraints Think of them as the “configuration + personality + operating manual” for an agent.They tell the agent how to act, not what to think.# Researcher Agent ## Role You are a Research Agent. Your purpose is to gather information, verify it across multiple sources, extract relevant details, and produce structured, accurate summaries. You specialize in technical research, documentation analysis, and multi‑source synthesis. ## Capabilities - Perform web searches - Fetch and extract content from URLs - Compare and cross‑verify information - Produce structured reports and summaries - Cite sources when possible - Store notes and findings in files - Maintain a research log using memory tools ## Tools - brave_search - fetch - read_file - write_file - memory_store - memory_retrieve ## Planning Strategy 1. Clarify the research question. 2. Generate a search plan (queries + target sources). 3. Fetch and extract content. 4. Compare findings across multiple sources. 5. Summarize results with citations. 6. Store notes for future reference. ## Workflow Rules - Always verify information using at least two sources. - Prefer primary sources over secondary commentary. - If uncertain, explicitly state uncertainty. - Keep summaries concise unless asked otherwise. ## Safety - Do not fetch unsafe or suspicious URLs. - Do not store sensitive data without explicit user approval. # Planner Agent ## Role You are a Planning Agent. Your job is to break complex tasks into actionable steps, create workflows, evaluate dependencies, and produce clear execution plans. You specialize in decomposition, sequencing, and strategy. ## Capabilities - Multi-step planning - Workflow generation - Task decomposition - Timeline estimation - Dependency mapping - Saving plans to files ## Tools - write_file - read_file - memory_store - memory_retrieve ## Planning Strategy 1. Understand the user’s goal. 2. Break the goal into logical phases. 3. Decompose phases into actionable tasks. 4. Identify dependencies and potential blockers. 5. Produce a step-by-step plan. 6. Optionally create timelines or structured workflow documents. ## Workflow Rules - Plans must be realistic and actionable. - Avoid unnecessary complexity. - Ask for clarification if the goal is ambiguous. - Provide alternatives when appropriate. ## Safety - Do not plan harmful or illegal activities. - Do not assume user intent; ask when unclear. # Coder Agent ## Role You are a Coding Agent. Your job is to read, write, fix, refactor, and execute code. You specialize in debugging, architecture, iterative improvement, and repository analysis. ## Capabilities - Read and analyze code - Write new code - Refactor existing code - Run code and inspect output - Debug errors and propose fixes - Work with Git repositories - Document code and changes ## Tools - read_file - write_file - execute_code - git_diff - git_status - fetch # for API documentation or external references ## Planning Strategy 1. Read relevant files to understand the problem. 2. Propose a fix or implementation plan. 3. Modify or write code as needed. 4. Execute code or tests to validate changes. 5. Iterate until stable and error-free. 6. Document changes or create commits when appropriate. ## Workflow Rules - Never overwrite files without explicit confirmation. - Always explain code changes clearly. - If execution fails, analyze the error and retry. - Keep commits small, meaningful, and well-documented. ## Safety - Do not run destructive commands. - Do not modify files outside the designated workspace. Creating a plan like : Implement X feature Gather info on X feature Implement it Test it
Agents decide when to call tools. Example : fetch(url) read_file(path) sql_query(query) git_diff(commit) maps_directions(origin, destination)
Tools come from MCP servers or local harness tools.After a tool runs, the agent reads the output and continues/adjusts.for example : Tool output: "File not found" Agent: "Try alternative path" Agents store and retrieve long-term context.Example: store project goals remember user preferences track previous steps Agents decide what to do next without user input.Example: “I need to fetch more data.” “I should run the code to test it.” “I will rewrite the function to fix the error.”
item.guid: AI/Organized info/2 System Prompts and Tools/Agents & Agentic Behavior.md

item.title: Agentic
item.description:
made to be "left running" or "run in schedule" HermesAgent Full: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent
LangChain Local Agents full agentic tool orchestration
AutoGPT Local autonomous task execution
SuperAGI Local multi‑tool autonomous agents
OpenInterpreter agentic coding + shell execution
OpenHands Turn coding agents into org-wide automation
Downloads

item.title: Sampling Parameters
item.description: Temperature — controls randomness; higher = more varied/creative, lower = more deterministic/focused Top-K — only considers the K most likely next tokens, cutting off the long unlikely tail Top-P (nucleus) — only considers the smallest set of tokens whose combined probability reaches P, adapts dynamically unlike top-K's fixed count Min-P — discards tokens below a probability threshold relative to the top token, another dynamic alternative to top-K Repeat penalty — reduces the probability of tokens the model has already used recently, discourages loops/repetition Presence penalty — flat penalty applied once to any token that has appeared at all in the output so far, encourages topic diversity Frequency penalty — like presence penalty but scales with how many times a token has appeared, stronger discouragement the more it repeats Reasoning budget — (specific to a few models) caps how many tokens the model can spend on internal reasoning before it must produce a final answer Remember lots of times the harness will send the parameters in the prompt, setting these in llama.cpp is just so it defaults to itHere's a few lists "semi well made" to give some good examples, they should make some sense:

item.title: Example Settings for llama.cpp
item.description:
Here's an example launch configuration for llama.cpp that I use in a 8gb VRAM gpu + 32Gb RAM.set LIMEAIMODEL=D:\Models\mudler\Qwopus3.6-35B-A3B-v1-APEX-MTP-GGUF\Qwopus3.6-35B-A3B-v1-APEX-MTP-I-Compact.gguf set LIMEAIAPI_KEY=Apikey-12345 set LIMEAICTX=131072 wt.exe F:\AIstuff\llama.cpp\llama-server.exe -m "%LIMEAIMODEL_PATH%" ^ --host 127.0.0.1 ^ --port 8080 ^ --api-key "%LIMEAIAPI_KEY%" ^ --n-gpu-layers 99 ^ --threads 6 ^ --threads-batch 6 ^ --ctx-size %LIMEAICTX% ^ --n-cpu-moe 37 ^ --batch-size 2048 ^ --ubatch-size 1024 ^ --flash-attn on ^ --cache-type-k q8_0 ^ --cache-type-v q8_0 ^ --cache-type-k-draft q8_0 ^ --cache-type-v-draft q8_0 ^ --spec-type draft-mtp ^ --spec-draft-n-max 3 ^ --kv-offload ^ --jinja ^ --mlock ^ --parallel 1 ^ --metrics ^ --no-mmap ^ --cont-batching Lets go over them one by one.1st I set the variables so I can quickly change the model , context and apikey.These are where the llama-serve will serve the openAI compatible API.This is where you set your api key This is how many layers you want in the gpu , I have 99 cause it's "impossible" , forcing to trigger the --fit , you can just use --fit if you want ( --fit-target tells in Mb how much VRAM to leave to the system )as an example : "-fit on -fitt 1024" means "auto-fit everything across devices, but always leave at least 1024 MiB free on each."Lets go over them one by one.1st I set the variables so I can quickly change the model , context and apikey.These are where the llama-serve will serve the openAI compatible API.This is where you set your api key This is how many layers you want in the gpu , I have 99 cause it's "impossible" , forcing to trigger the --fit , you can just use --fit if you want ( --fit-target tells in Mb how much VRAM to leave to the system )as an example : "-fit on -fitt 1024" means "auto-fit everything across devices, but always leave at least 1024 MiB free on each."Number of CPU threads used for token generation ( --threads ) and prompt processing ( --threads-batch )This is the |Context size , in that example it's 128k (131072) adjust it to use more or less VRAMThis tells his how many experts go to the cpu , adjust this to use more or less VRAM. The more experts in the cpu "the better" , but they will use more memory.Batch size is the max number of tokens processed together during prompt ingestion ubatch size is the physical sub-batch size actually sent to the GPU per step. Bigger values speed up prompt processing but use more VRAM/compute buffer , but they give more SPEEDDDDEnables Flash Attention, a faster and more memory-efficient way to compute attention. Required if you want to use quantized KV cache without a speed penalty
Sets the Kv Cache format for the Key and Value cache respectively (f16, q8_0, q4_0, etc.).
Same idea, but for the Draft model own KV cache when using speculative decoding (MTP or a separate draft model)
This turns on MTP Draft and sets it to try to "guess" 3 tokens in the future.
Controls whether the Kv Cache lives in GPU VRAM alongside the model layers, or CPU/system RAM. (Enabled by default)
The KV cache stays on-GPU for speed, since GPU-Local access avoids shuttling data across PCIe on every token.This flag tells the CLI to output or use the model’s chat template in raw Jinja format, instead of the processed/default prompt format. So it can use tools
Note: Qwen models usually improve by using this : https://huggingface.co/froggeric/Qwen-Fixed-Chat-Templates/tree/main downloading the chat_template.jinja , and using --jinja --chat-template-file chat_template.jinja ^Locks the model into physical RAM, preventing the OS from swapping it to disk. Avoids stutters from page faults, but requires you to actually have enough free RAM to hold it locked.Number of concurrent request "slots" the server can handle at once. 1 means single-user, sequential requests only Raise it if you want to serve multiple simultaneous conversations, at the cost of splitting your KV cache budget across slots.Exposes a Prometheus-compatible metrics endpoint on the server for monitoring things like token throughput and request stats over time.Disables memory-mapped model loading. Normally mmap lets the OS lazily page in the model file, which speeds up load and reduces peak RAM use, disabling it forces the whole model to load into RAM upfront, slower to start but can avoid page-fault stutter mid-inference on some systems.Enables continuous batching : the server can dynamically mix new incoming requests into an in-progress generation batch instead of waiting for the current batch to finish, improving throughput when serving multiple requests. ( actually irrelevant in this config since parallel 1 )
Enables ngram based speculative decoding instead of a model-based drafter (like MTP Draft or a separate draft model). Instead of running any neural network to guess upcoming tokens, it pattern-matches against recent contextSets the maximum n-gram size (lookback window) used for pattern matching to 64.
You can use this and MTP Draft at the same time, but sometimes it introduces slowdowns.
item.guid: AI/Organized info/5 Performance/Example Settings for llama.cpp.md

item.title: Docker Info,filesystem,cleanup
item.description: docker exec -it opencode ash or docker exec -it opencode bashIn this case I'm "opening" the opencode container , change the name for your case.docker run -d --name cf-3333 cloudflare/cloudflared:latest tunnel --url http://host.docker.internal:3333this will create and give you a cloudflare hostname exposing your port 3333 to the internetTo check docker disk usage docker system df -vTo clean the build cache ( no problem doing this , it will just make next installation slower ) docker builder prune"dangling images" are images that aren't connected , sometimes when you update a docker it will leave "old versions" you can check by doing this docker images -f "dangling=true" and docker image prune to clean themDocker creates a filesystem in your drive ( you can sellect where in docker desktop )This file system grows larger , but never "shrinks" , it's a problem with WSL2This is how to Shrink the docker file, I do recommend you to do this after you got everything setup.Turn off docker On the cmd line :wsl --shutdown Then do diskpart Then in diskpart:select vdisk file="D:\Docker path to Filesystem\DockerDesktopWSL\disk\docker_data.vhdx" attach vdisk readonly compact vdisk detach vdisk exit Obviously change the D:\Docker path to Filesystem\DockerDesktopWSL\disk\docker_data.vhdx , to your correct file

item.title: API Management & Routing
item.description: You might want to use a routing system , so it automatically changes from any local system to onlineAPI.I personally do this manually LiteLLM is an open source gateway. It exposes a single OpenAI compatible API endpoint that routes requests to over 100 different LLM providers. These providers include OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Together AI, and local servers like llama.cpp.You can run LiteLLM as a lightweight local proxy or as a Docker container. Its memory footprint is small, roughly 65 MB, and the added latency per request is under 1 millisecond.note : LiteLLM has built in caching with backends for memory, disk, and Redis. It supports semantic caching when using Redis or Valkey as the vector store. You can run Valkey in a local Docker container to enable this without any cloud dependency.This is "kinda" illegal , but there is a system that "hijacks" the anti-gravity models to other CLIs It also can use several accounts and jump from one to another as soon as the limits reach.DO NOT USE YOUR MAIN GOOGLE ACCOUNT WITH THIS

item.title: Program and Sources
item.description:
llama.cpp
ik_llama.cpp
Ollama
Vllm
LMStudio ( windows only ) Notes: llama.cpp is the mainstream ik_llama.cpp is a fork usually has better cpu performance but "lags behind" in some other applications ollama has it's own distribution , and is "simpler" ( If your tarded ) vllm has better support for multigpu and "serving a lot of ppl ( 10+ ) in 1 box" Lm Studio has lower performance (like ollama), but good for testing models.
Docker Desktop
LibreChat : https://www.librechat.ai/docs
(PewDiePie mod)Odyseeus : https://github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus
OpenWebUI : https://github.com/open-webui/open-webui

Jan.ai

Clinea - ( use vs.code extentions to install )
Continue.dev - ( use vs.code extentions to install )
CodeGPT - ( use vs.code extentions to install )
Cursor
Antigravity : does not work with local models , to use google models These can be used also as Agentic Coding:
Opencode
Pi
ClaudeCode
Aider
HermesAgent : https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent

Local LangChain Agents
Opencode
Pi
Aider

awesome-agents

item.guid: AI/Organized info/8 Downloads and websites/Program and Sources.md

item.title: The Software Stack
item.description:
The way an LLM runs is basicaly:{Model itself} ----API---->{Harness}So installing a Harness you can connect to Online API List or a Local run AI and use it.
Fell free to also use other dockers , for example , we can install "seaXNG" as a docker container.{Model itself running locally} ---API--->{Docker with Harness}--->{Docker SeaXNG}Giving our harness the tool to "search the web" , we can also run MCP servers and others using this.You can run a local model for coding , and run an onlineAPI for review and bug finding.You can mix and match local , controled-hosting , onlineAPI at will
I do have to always warn to run the harness in a virtualized space ( docker for example ) to avoid the AI doing some weird shit in your system.

item.title: Prompt Engineering
item.description:
Prompt engineering is the practice of designing inputs that reliably produce the outputs you want from an LLM. It’s not about “magic words”.It’s about structure, constraints, and clarity. Good prompts make models predictable, controllable, and efficient.You also want to give it all relevant information and context , when asking in opencode about your code , opencode WILL send that code to the AI , giving it context.On direct talks , we need to do it manually. Be explicit. Use structure. Use constraints. Avoid ambiguity. Prefer short prompts (as short as you can) over long ones. Use examples when formatting matters. Use output structuring for predictable results. Use agent‑style prompting for autonomous workflows. Test prompts with multiple models. Iterate. Claude Prompt Guide
OpenAI Prompt guide
Promptingguide.ai
PromptHero
Awesome Prompt Engineering Here are some prompt ideas , you can use several , but gives a general idea.Define who the model is and what expertise it should emulate.Pattern: You are a <role>. You specialize in <domain>. Adopt the mindset and reasoning style of a professional. Example : "You are a senior software engineer specializing in debugging Python applications."Give explicit, direct instructions.Pattern: Your task is to <do X> Follow the instructions exactly. Do not add extra commentary. Example : "Your task is to rewrite the function for clarity and performance."Limit the output format or behavior.Pattern: Output only in <format>. No additional text. Keep the answer under <N> words. Example : "Output only valid JSON. No prose."Force structured thinking before answering.Pattern: Break the solution into steps. Explain reasoning briefly. Then provide the final answer. Example: "Think step‑by‑step before giving the final solution."Show examples to teach the model the desired pattern.Pattern: Example Input → Example Output pairs Then provide a new input Example: Example Input: “Sort: 3,1,2” Example Output: “1,2,3” Now sort: 9,4,7Tell the model when and how to use external capabilities.Pattern: If you need external data, call the appropriate tool. Explain why you are calling the tool. After receiving the result, evaluate it before continuing. Example: "If you need more information, call the fetch tool."Used in agent.md / claude.md definitions.Pattern: Follow a think → act → observe loop. After each action, evaluate the result. Stop when the goal is achieved. Ask for clarification if the objective is ambiguous. Example: "Think about the next best action before calling any tool."Prevent unwanted behavior.*Pattern: Do not perform actions outside the defined scope Ask for clarification if the request is unclear or unsafe. Never assume missing details. Example: "If the user request is ambiguous, ask for clarification."Tell the model how to behave when something goes wrong.Pattern: Identify the cause. Propose a fix. Retry the solution. Example: "If an error occurs, explain the cause and attempt a corrected version."Force predictable formatting.Example : Respond using this exact structure:## SUMMARY <one paragraph> ## DETAILS - <bullet point> - <bullet point> - <bullet point> ## OUTPUT <final answer in required format> ## CHECK - Requirements met: Yes/No - Constraints respected: Yes/No - Uncertainties: <list> Ask the model to generate multiple reasoning paths and choose the best one.Example: "Generate three different approaches. Select the most accurate one. Provide only the final answer."Prevent hallucinations.Example: "If you are unsure, say “I am not certain.” Do not invent facts. Ask for clarification when needed."
item.guid: AI/Organized info/2 System Prompts and Tools/Prompt Engineering.md

item.title: Context & Kv Cache
item.description:
Context is what you ( or your Harness ) send to the AI.
In a Basic task if you ask your Harness to "check my emails" , the Harness will send the emails in the prompt.If the AI needs to search a program's library to learn how it works, that documentation gets pulled in and added to the context too.
This matters because context isn't free every token in it costs processing time and (on local models especially) memory via the Kv Cache.A Harness that dumps in more than it needs slows things down and can push relevant information further from the model's "attention," even before you hit the model's context limitDo note that some models do have problems with context before their limit.You might see a model with max 256k context , that struggles after 120k context and gets "lost".When you reach the limit , the model will just bork, so most programs when reaching a limit will try to "compact" the Context and save only relevant thing.When a model processes text, each token gets converted into two internal representations inside every attention layer : a Key Value vector These are what the model uses to figure out how much attention each token should pay to every other token that came before it.Without a cache, the model would have to recompute the Key and Value vectors for the entire conversation from scratch on every single new token.The KV cache stores these vectors as they're generated, so each new token only needs its own K/V computed once, then reuses everything already cached from before.This is what makes autoregressive generation (one token at a time) fast.
But it comes at a cost: the cache grows with every token in your Context, and it lives in VRAM/RAM right alongside the model weights. Long context = a big KV cache
This is also why KV cache Quantization exists: you can compress the K and V vectors down from 16-bit to lower precision to fit longer context in the same VRAM, at some small cost to quality.Though I do not recomend going bellow Q8
item.guid: AI/Organized info/3 Models Info/Context & Kv Cache.md

item.title: RAG tools
item.description: Retrieval‑Augmented Generation (RAG) is a way to give your model more info. creative writing emotional conversation roleplay coding tasks requiring reasoning math proofs chain-of-thought reasoning factual answers documentation codebases research enterprise knowledge When you want to create a group of files , for the model to read and have access it will use RAG, for example : If you run OpenWebUI and give it 30pdfsOpenWebUI will : embed your PDFs chunk them ( cut them in pieces) store them in a local vector database retrieve relevant chunks when you ask a question feed those chunks into llama.cpp So most "WebUI" already have a RAG systemOdysseus uses chromeDB to store the RAG system. https://www.llamaindex.ai/
item.guid: AI/Organized info/2 System Prompts and Tools/RAG tools.md

item.title: Finetunes
item.description: Finetunes are customized versions of a base model that have been trained further on a specific style, domain, or behavior. Instead of building a new model from scratch, a finetune takes an existing foundation model ( like Qwen, Llama, or DeepSeek )and “specializes” it using additional data. This extra training can shape how the model speaks, how it reasons or how it performs in coding tasks. Because the base model already understands language and structure, finetuning is far cheaper and faster than full training while still producing dramatic changes in personality and capability. Finetunes are popular in the local‑LLM community because they let creators build models tailored for specific use cases: expressive character chat, high‑precision coding, jailbreak‑resistant assistants, or stylistic personas like Qwopus or Ornith.Even though these finetunes may feel like entirely new models, they’re still built on top of the original architecture and inherit its strengths and limitations. The finetune simply reshapes how the model behaves, responds, and prioritizes information.As example of finetunes to the Qwen3.6 35B we have Qwopus and OrnithSome people also check the "locks" the AI has , and run it uncensored , removing said locks , it seems they are more prone to hallucinations , but when a model refuses to teach you how to "hack the memory of a game" , these finetunes might help ( but it seems most of the comunity uses uncensored builds for porn )There are also different quantization tricks.

item.title: Benchmarks and efficiency
item.description: Published benchmark scores are the least reliable part of any model card.Contamination (test sets leaking into training data), cherry-picked prompting templates, and benchmark-specific fine-tuning ("benchmaxxing") mean a model can top a leaderboard and still feel worse than a lower-scoring model in your actual workflow. Treat every benchmark number as a hypothesis to verify, not a conclusion.For your coding specifically, the benchmark that actually matters is SWE-bench Verified (real GitHub issues, real repo context, requires actual patch generation + test passing) These matter more than MMLU/HumanEval (agentic coding stack, OpenCode/Cline, real repo work).Reading these correctly: SWE-bench Verified is your best single proxy for "will this model be a good daily driver for agentic coding" Watch for score inflation via harness tuning. Some labs report SWE-bench numbers using heavily engineered scaffolds (multiple attempts, self-verification loops, custom retrieval) that don't match what you'll get running raw through OpenCode. Always check whether the number is "model alone" or "model + elaborate harness." Edit-format compliance (Aider-style) is a separate skill from coding ability. A model can reason through the right fix and still fail because it can't emit a diff your tool can parse, this is the same failure class as an agentic JSON tool-call issue, just for code edits instead of function calls. None of these measure long-loop degradation well except METR-style benchmarks. SWE-bench is single-shot-ish (one issue, one patch). IF your multi-critic harness runs many turns, that's a different reliability axis almost no public benchmark captures , which is why your own eval set still matters more than any leaderboard. A few points of difference is noise, not signal. 73.4% vs 71% on SWE-bench Verified is within run-to-run variance territory for many harnesses. Don't chase small deltas. LLM-as-judge benchmarks (MT-Bench, Arena-Hard) measure "sounds good to another LLM," not correctness. They correlate reasonably with human preference for chat, but are weak signals for coding/agentic/factual tasks. Static benchmarks miss something: tool-call JSON reliability, instruction adherence over long agentic loops, context handling at your working context length, tokens/sec at your hardware (none of which MMLU/HumanEval touch at all). Two models with identical benchmark scores can differ hugely in practice Treat leaderboards as a shortlist filter, not a ranking. Use them to cut a field of 50 models down to 5 Rule of thumb: benchmarks tell you what's plausible , use lmarena or make your own tests.

item.title: Good Papers
item.description:
https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.11903
https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.11171
https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629
https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.04761

item.title: Local
item.description:
I personally recommend to use a simple "loader" like LM studio on windows to test/search models and then use llama.cpp to test actual performance , if your in linux just go straight to llama.cppTest some models , if your goal is to have a chat assistant , a coding assistant, or an Agentic Coding make some tests to test it's performance, both in quality of answer and speed.Right now as I'm writing this , for my system ( 8gb VRAM + 32GB VRAM ) it's hard to beat Qwen3.6 35B MoE or it's finetunes ( Qwopus , ornith , etc ) for my use cases.For me personally I can get arround 50-60tok/s with qwen3.6 35B with 128k context , I find it very acceptable due to my hardware constrains.I recommend to most local users to use MoE models, since most of us aren't swimming in 64GB VRAM gpus .... ( sadly )
Look at how much RAM+VRAM you have to spare , how much context you want , and pick your models.
Grab llama.cpp and/or LM studio from the Downloads and start testing
I left an example llama.cpp launch hereWhen using any sort of harness that allows to run code/script ( pewdiepie odysseus , opencode , etc ) I recommend using docker to have a "virtual space" for the AIThis avoid issues , since the giving the AI access to run code/shell in your main OS CAN RUIN YOUR SYSTEM .I personally have llama.cpp running native Docker running opencode pi pewdiepie odysseus librechat So if opencode does something really bad , it just ruins it's docker container.

item.title: General Chat
item.description:
These focus on conversation, safety, and general reasoning. They do not enforce strict code formatting or agentic planning. LibreChat 2 : ChatGPT‑style interface, multi‑backend, websearch
(PewDiePie mod)Odyseeus 2 : chat harness, websearch
OpenWebUI 2 : local web UI, multi‑model chat, tool plugins, websearch
SillyTavern 2 : character‑chat harness, RP‑focused, websearch ( the comunity has a lot of ... RPers ....)
Jan.ai offline ChatGPT‑like desktop app
LM Studio local chat + model management, simple chat
Downloads

item.title: Agentic Coding
item.description:
These support multi‑step reasoning, planning, and tool usage, but stay focused on coding tasks. HermesAgent 2 : agentic coding with planning loops
Continue.dev Agent Mode planning + tool calls
SillyTavern Tools Mode 2 : agentic extensions
Local LangChain Agents tool‑driven coding agents
Opencode since it can plan->run->test it can also be a full agentic coding
Pi since it can plan->run->test it can also be a full agentic coding
Aider
Downloads

item.title: Coding
item.description:
These can be usefull to help with coding , understand codebases , be your assistant while codingVS.code Pluggins : Clinea - vs.code plugin , quite decent , I use this ( use vs.code extentions to install )
Continue.dev - vs.code plugin ( use vs.code extentions to install )
CodeGPT - ( use vs.code extentions to install )
Cursor : vs.code fork made for AI
Antigravity : does not work with local models , to use google models These can be used also as Agentic Coding:
Opencode Light , decent , I use this
Pi uses much less context , I use this
ClaudeCode uses a LOT of context
Aider didn't test much , "git native"
Downloads

item.title: My personal Setup
item.description:
Right now (06/2026) I'm testing and using on my system ( 8gb VRAM+ 32GB RAM) Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-APEX-MTP-I-Compact.gguf
ornith-1.0-35b-APEX-I-Compact-MTP.gguf
Qwopus3.6-35B-A3B-v1-APEX-MTP-I-Compact.gguf
Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf ( as backup honestly ) You can also look at Gemma 25B A4B
Gemma4-26B-A4B-QAT-Uncensored-HauhauCS-Balanced-Q4_K_M.gguf I'm sure this list will be outdated very soon.
I run llama.cpp locally with these settings I get around 55-65tok/s in code.It exposes the API in my pc ( localhost ) port 8080Currently I have these docker containers : Pewdiepie Odysseus ( comes with seaxng ) Opencode Pi Librechat Cloudflare tunel to "expose" my AI/harness to the internet Or I just use vs.code+ pluggin to connect to it.
I load the harness I want, all my harnesses ( unless it's a vs.code extention ) are running using docker
I load the docker container , and I setup the api endpoint ( usually it's already done)For an example of a docker container , here's my dockerfile.opencode :# Stage 1: Build snip binary FROM golang:alpine AS builder RUN go install github.com/edouard-claude/snip/cmd/snip@latest || \ go install github.com/VincentHardouin/opencode-snip/cmd/snip@latest # Stage 2: Build codebase-memory-mcp from source (Alpine/musl compatible) FROM alpine:latest AS cbm-builder RUN apk add --no-cache \ git \ gcc \ g++ \ make \ musl-dev \ zlib-dev \ bash RUN cd /tmp && \ git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/DeusData/codebase-memory-mcp.git && \ cd codebase-memory-mcp && \ ./scripts/build.sh # Stage 3: Final image FROM ghcr.io/anomalyco/opencode:latest RUN apk add --no-cache \ git \ bash \ curl \ wget \ nodejs \ npm \ python3 \ py3-pip \ ripgrep \ zlib # FIX: Bypass peer dependency conflict (upstream @opentui/core version mismatch) RUN npm install -g @tarquinen/opencode-dcp --legacy-peer-deps # Copy from /go/bin (not /root/go/bin) COPY --from=builder /go/bin/snip /usr/local/bin/opencode-snip RUN ln -s /usr/local/bin/opencode-snip /usr/local/bin/snip # Copy musl-built codebase-memory-mcp binary COPY --from=cbm-builder /tmp/codebase-memory-mcp/build/c/codebase-memory-mcp /usr/local/bin/codebase-memory-mcp RUN chmod +x /usr/local/bin/codebase-memory-mcp ENV PATH="/usr/local/bin:$PATH" This will make a linux image to build every tool needed ( even a memory mcp server ) , then copy it to another linux image ( final one ) and sets up opencode and extra tools for it to work ( bash , git , zlib , npm , wget )I just go to the folder where it is and run "docker build -t opencode"For example if we wanted it to use gcc , we can just ADD gcc to the list and it will be included in the final image.Then I load opencode with a comand like this : wt.exe docker run -it --rm --name opencode ^ -v "D:\AI projects\opencode:/workspace" ^ -v "D:\AI projects\opencode-config:/root/.config/opencode" ^ -v "D:\AI projects\opencode-data:/root/.local/share/opencode" ^ -v "D:\AI projects\opencode-cbm-cache:/root/.cache/codebase-memory-mcp" ^ -w /workspace ^ opencode-full I use windows terminal ( wt.exe ) cause it looks better and handles mouse/keyboard better.Then I'm "binding" folders meaning that for example : "D:\AI projects\opencode-config" the windows folder will be in the linux image : "/root/.config/opencode" Do I can go to the "D:\AI projects\opencode-config" folder and just edit the configuration."D:\AI projects\opencode" is binded to "/workspace"meaning I place my files in "D:\AI projects\opencode" and now that docker will see them and interact with them.This means opencode CAN't mess with anything other than those folders.

item.title: But...why...
item.description: LLM models are advancing pretty fast , this guide was written in June 2026 , as you read it , it probably will be outdated , new things have arrivedIf we can envision a perfect scenario were and LLM doesn't fail , we can envision the perfect "SIRI HELP" scenario.Asking an AI to check our downloads , telling it to download X things so we have it when we arrive home, asking it to check out schedules and friend lists and call everyone for a party , analysing documents news ...It will be a great helper.As when things changed in the world ( digital art , digital photography , digital games ... etc ) , be it good or bad , one has to go with the flow to keep up his "performance".AI was one of the biggest intellectual thefts ever made , probably the number1 , I do not think in history , even with great pillaging's , we never saw so much intellectual property and data being "stolen" and used "for free"I think it's clear I think this was stolen , meta DID steal those books , anthropic/openAI did STEAL a lot of things ... but I cannot stop the world, and trying to do so in an area , would just "handicap" it in this field , when others do not follow these rules.One of the biggest reasons why AI models are mostly Chinese/USA , is due to the EU laws on LLMs... even though I think EU is correct , you can't compete like this.As such as an individual I must adapt, and right now I do believe LLMs are a big tool.Due to the amazing capabilities of these helpers/assistants , I do believe most companies will try to make us use it and be dependent , just for later to start asking for money for features.It's like me giving you a car for free , but after sometime I ask you 1€ to turn it on , at this point you were counting on it ... you will pay , you are DEPENDANT.For this grim future.... Local is here to help , both to provide a "minimum free service" that companies can't go under ( since ppl wont pay for something that is free ) and need to offer it and only ask for money for "better than local"They are and will be used to track you , do you have a long talking history with a model ? ( GPT , claude etc ) , try asking it "What can you infeer about me from your memories" ...In fact , the AI model knowing about you and how you like .. data , how you like to talk , do you like to "assume stuff" or are you very "strick" , do you enjoy knowing HOW things work , or just "what to do to make it work" .... this is the basis of a good harnessOne of the things that makes a model "feel" better is based on it knowing what YOU want, data retrieval and user profiling IS NEEDED.If you now make code and send it to review to claude,openAI,deepseek ... you can be sure that code will be used for training data.And I do expect politics to be involved, a country making an AI can control who can use it , or use it to track who uses it using stenography and tricks almost impossible to see by a normal human.IN FACT ... fable5 the way it "saves the current date" tells anthropic who is running it, for a user these symbols are the same ' ' ʼ ʹ but .. they are not ... and they are used to track who is using anthropic AI . sources 1 2Local keeps your data , yours, and avoids someone suddenly taking something you have and denying access and/or asking for money.

item.title: I don't wanna run a model on my pc it gets hot!
item.description:
I totally get this , and there are other options...You can just run an harness and point to an Online API List or a Self-hosted model , this by itself gives you MUCH MORE CONTROL over the model.Most users are interfacing with models with an automatic system , invisible to them, they have little control over the AI settings like temperature , or system prompt.This is great for non technical users, and I do believe most advanced interfaces ( claude ) , do adapt their system prompt to your question.
But having your own Harness helps a LOT to understand, control and direct the model to where you want it to be.
item.guid: AI/Organized info/0 Ramblings/I don't wanna run a model on my pc it gets hot!.md

item.title: But ... I dont have a 5000€ mac or pc
item.description:
I don't either, and I'll be honest, when I looked at this my 1st time, most models I could run were quite ... retarded...tool or agentic workflow were not even feasible things, it would forget or not call it correctly.They could do basic text rewriting, search and information ... but they were really badThen due to the improvement models even lower than 10Billion parameters , if given access to websearch, could give decent answers and be used as Chat models.This was great , but the tok/s speed was not great in my little war machine, even so, worth it to explore a field like this.Most of the time , going over VRAM would mean sub 2tok/s speeds.With improvements to quantization and expecially MoE , we can now run offload models ( VRAM+RAM ) without going to abysmal speeds.A user with 6-12GB VRAM + RAM , can now run a 35B parameter with high context , at very reasonable speeds !!
MTP Draft helps a LOT in coding , and is being somewhat ignored , since most ppl that I talk to with 5090s or RTX 6000 , loose speed with MTP Draft , but for users with VRAM issues it really helps a LOT.I suspect things will improve even more , local is gaining a lot of traction.
item.guid: AI/Organized info/0 Ramblings/But ... I dont have a 5000€ mac or pc.md

item.title: System Prompts
item.description:
A system prompt is the foundational instruction that defines how an LLM should behave before any user message is ever seen. It sets the model’s role, constraints, tone, reasoning style, and allowed actions. Unlike normal prompts, the system prompt acts as a behavioral contract: it tells the model what it is, what it must prioritize, and what it must avoid. Everything the model does ( tool calling, reasoning depth, autonomy, formatting ) flows from this initial framing.In practice, a system prompt is the “operating rules” for a Harness.
A chat Harness uses it to enforce clarity and safety; a coding harness uses it to enforce determinism and strict formatting; an agentic harness uses it to define planning, tool usage, and autonomy boundaries. Because LLMs are highly sensitive to system‑level instructions, a well‑designed system prompt is the difference between a stable assistant and one that hallucinates, misuses tools, or behaves unpredictably.
item.guid: AI/Organized info/2 System Prompts and Tools/System Prompts.md

item.title: DRAFT Dflash MTP future prediction
item.description: A Draft system explained simply is when you have something that writes fast, and trying to "guess", then if it guesses correctly the AI simply writes faster.As a decent way to see it , imagine an AI with 30B parameters, and now a small AI attached with 1B parameters, and we let the second AI "Try to guess" the next 4 words.If we get an ok acceptance rate ( 0.90 , 0.80 , 0.70 , 0.60 ) this would give us a LOT of extra speed.It really comes handy in coding, since it's easy "looking back" to guess what we'r doing, a lot of repetition and syntax.To simplify all of these "predicts" are measured by "acceptance rate" , you usually loose performance, but then gain it, when the "draft model" accurately predicts tokens in front.We have to balance time spent predicting a maxdraft of 16 would create a LOT of negatives , thus probably lowering our tok/s by a LOT.We run a lighter model , same family to try to predict the future tokens.Instead of a separate model, the target model itself has extra lightweight prediction heads trained to predict tokens sharing the same backbone/hidden states. This is baked into the architecture at training time (DeepSeek-V3 popularized this). Because it's self-contained, there's no separate draft model to manage, and acceptance rates tend to be higher since it's the same model's "opinion" about future tokens, just from a "shallower" head.Quite "new" draft-dflash uses a small draft model that reads the target model's hidden states to predict the next tokens, reaching higher acceptance than a standalone draft model.I haven't tested this much since it's not implemented into llama.cpp ( should be VERY SOON )Recently released from deepseek , currently only for deepseek V4 ( when you read this maybe it's out =)) )ngram-mod is here cause I dunno where to put it , you can use it in llama.cpp , and it basically tries to predict the next 32 or 64 tokens, sometimes it gives stalls, so test with your system. --spec-type ngram-mod ^ --spec-ngram-mod-n-max 32 ^
item.guid: AI/Organized info/5 Performance/DRAFT Dflash MTP future prediction.md

item.title: Model Sources
item.description: HuggingFace
item.guid: AI/Organized info/8 Downloads and websites/Model Sources.md

item.title: List of Online Models
item.guid: AI/Organized info/0 Ramblings/List of Online Models.md

item.title: MCP servers
item.description: MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers are programs that expose capabilities to an AI model through a standardized interface. They act as the “tool layer” of an agentic system, the part that lets the model do things instead of just generating text.They provide three types of capabilities: Tools : active operations the model can call (write files, query APIs, search GitHub). Resources : passive read-only data (file contents, database schemas, calendars). Application Prompts : prebuilt instruction templates for workflows (summarize meetings, plan trips). MCP servers are not part of the model, they are not part of the system prompt, and are not a part of the Harness. Think of them like "servers" that connect to your AI and give them tools and/or knowledge on how to run the tools.They can be local , to give access to a filesystem , or a specific program.There is a "blender-mcp server" , and connecting your AI to it , lets it "control blender" trough the mcp server.There are online MCP servers , here's some examples ( they need an API key )Brave MCP : wss://mcp.brave.com/search Gives you search on your model and teaches it how to use it. Google maps : wss://mcp.googleapis.com/maps Now your AI knows how to read maps , give directions This should make you understand what is an MCP ... there is an unrealengine mcp , a sqlmcp , a lot of mcp servers are out there , and it's a great way to give your model tools.
item.guid: AI/Organized info/2 System Prompts and Tools/MCP servers.md

item.title: Tool and function calling
item.description: Takeaway: Models differ dramatically in how they expose tools. Your system prompt must define the tool protocol, error behavior, and fallback strategy.Note : in llama.cpp you should use --jinja Note: Qwen models usually improve by using this : https://huggingface.co/froggeric/Qwen-Fixed-Chat-Templates/tree/main downloading the chat_template.jinja , and using --jinja --chat-template-file chat_template.jinja ^ JSON‑native models (Llama, Qwen, DeepSeek): Expect strict JSON schema. Fail silently if fields are missing or types mismatch. XML‑style tool callers (older OpenAI, some proprietary setups): Require wrapper tags like <tool> or <function_call>. Hybrid models: Accept JSON but require a wrapper (e.g., "tool": { ... }"). Your system prompt must explicitly define: Tool invocation rules (“Only call tools when explicitly instructed.”) Schema strictness (“Follow the exact JSON schema provided.”) Error handling (“If a tool call fails or schema is unclear, ask for clarification.”) Verification loop (“After receiving tool output, verify correctness before continuing.”)
item.guid: AI/Organized info/2 System Prompts and Tools/Tool and function calling.md

item.title: Hosted‑but‑self‑run models
item.description:
I do this sometimes , This means you control a server , and you install the model in it.This means you still control the model and it's data , and it's all "clear" as soon as you disable the server. I do use some "free options" ( but if you have money you can just buy/rent a server ) Kaggle https://www.kaggle.com/
Google colab : https://colab.research.google.com/ Kaggle gives you a 2xT4 ( 2x16GB + 32-50 RAM ) , I think about 20h per week Colab gives you a 1xT4 ( 16Gb +12 RAM ) , I really dunno the limit.This is what I recommend for setting them up : You make a model selection cell with extra parameters : Select model ( and proper model location to download from hugging face ) Context limit ubatch+batch ( for tuning speed ) mmap on/off ( for tuning speed )
KV cache Quantization options MTP DRAFT and max draft limit ( speed tunning ) Jinja template on or off ( for coding ) Threads ( for tuning )
Sampling Parameters API key (security) Then those values are passed to cell2 , cell 2 downloads llama.cpp , sets up everything uses the cell1 info to download model and run llama.cppCell3 should be a proxy to expose it outside , there are some ways to do this : ngrok Cloudflare tunels zgrok Nport TunnelAPI instatunnel Loophole Make it output the API endpoint + key , so you can easily setup. some of these offer random endpoints , others have custom ( always the same )Then just connect your harness to it.

item.title: llama.cpp metrics reading
item.description: When running llama.cpp you will get a lot of info.check the prefill token/s ( how fast it reads the prompt) and the answer token/s ( this is usually much more important )Take a look at the draft "acceptance" , an MTP draft getting 0.40 acceptance in 1st token , is a waste of time.

item.title: Online API List
item.description:
There are a few "free" Online APIs , I will ignore the ones with "300$ credit" , and focus on the ones that have rate limits /day or week or month.The models they offer change , and their rates too , new providers appear , so keep an eye out.Providers like deepseek and other do give a "credit" on new account , but once it's over ... it's over. Google ( gemini ) - https://aistudio.google.com/app/apikey ( apiendpoint : https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai )
Mistral - https://admin.mistral.ai/organization/api-keys ( apiendpoint : https://api.mistral.ai/v1 )
OpenRouter - https://openrouter.ai/ ( apiendpoint : https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 )
Groq - https://console.groq.com/keys ( apiendpoint : https://api.groq.com/openai/v1 )
Cerebras - https://inference-docs.cerebras.ai/console/api-keys ( apiendpoint : https://api.cerebras.ai/v1 )
GitHub Models - https://github.com/settings/profile ( apiendpoint : https://models.inference.ai.azure.com )
NVIDIA NIM - https://developer.nvidia.com/nim ( apiendpoint : https://integrate.api.nvidia.com/v1 )
LLM7.io - https://llm7.io/ ( apiendpoint : https://api.llm7.io/v1 )
OVHcloud Free endpoint : https://oai.endpoints.kepler.ai.cloud.ovh.net/v1
Cohere : https://cohere.com/ ( apiendpoint : https://api.cohere.com/v2/chat )
Z.ai (GLM) : https://chat.z.ai/ ( apiendpoint: https://api.z.ai/api/paas/v4/ )
Pollinations AI : https://pollinations.ai/ ( apiendpoint : https://gen.pollinations.ai/v1/chat/completions )
BazaarLink.ai : https://bazaarlink.ai/ ( apiendpoint : https://bazaarlink.ai/api/v1 ) Do remember that tools like cursor or antigravity do give their own "free limited" models, and usually are good choices.You can connect your harness to them , or use an agentic system that the coder is local , but the reviewer is one of these.

item.title: What is an harness
item.description:
A harness is the program that sits between you and the model : it takes your input, builds the actual prompt the model sees, sends it off for inference, and does something with the model's response.If you've only used something like ChatGPT or Claude's chat interface, this distinction is easy to miss, because the harness is invisible, the chat app is the harness, quietly doing a lot of work you never see: It adds a system prompt you never typed, telling the model how to behave It formats your message into whatever chat template the model expects (special tokens marking who said what) It keeps track of the conversation history and re-sends the relevant parts every time, since the model itself has no memory between requests If you attach a file or it does a web search, the harness fetches that content and stuffs it into context before the model ever sees your question If the AI can use tools (search the web, run code, check your calendar), the harness is the thing that actually executes those tool calls and feeds the results back in the model itself can't do anything, it can only say what it wants to do. When you're running a model through something like Claude or ChatGPT, all of this is baked in and tuned by the company that built it. But ... The moment you run a model locally, you become responsible for choosing (or building) the harness and different harnesses are built for very different jobs: A General Chat UI (Open WebUI, LM Studio's chat) just handles conversation history and formatting
A Coding assistant (like an IDE plugin) adds your open files and project structure into context automatically
An Agentic Coding harness (OpenCode, Claude Code-style tools) goes much further it can read/write files, run terminal commands, call APIs, and loop the model through multiple steps on its own, deciding what to do with each result before deciding what to do next
An Agentic harness is the same as an Agentic Coding harness but focused on other things than coding , it might just check your email , check the news, stocks , and make reports on it every 6h . Same underlying model, wildly different behavior — because the harness controls what the model can see and what it's allowed to do with what it says. This is also why a model that's great at chat can feel clumsy in an agentic harness, or vice versa: the harness has to match the model's actual strengths (tool-calling format, context handling, etc.), which is exactly what the next few sections in this guide are about.
item.guid: AI/Organized info/1 Harness/What is an harness.md

item.title: System Prompt for Harness
item.description: A system‑prompt design must match the harness it powers, because each harness expects a different style of reasoning, autonomy, and output discipline. A chat harness needs a light, conversational frame that emphasizes correctness, clarity, and uncertainty handling.A coding harness needs strict rules: deterministic formatting, no hallucinated APIs, and minimal prose. An agentic coding harness requires a deeper framing that instructs the model to plan, break tasks into steps, verify assumptions, and use tools deliberately.A full agentic harness needs the strongest framing of all—explicit autonomy boundaries, confirmation rules, and a clear separation between planning and acting.If the system prompt doesn’t match the harness, the model drifts: chat models start acting agentic, coding models start explaining instead of producing code, and agentic models skip planning or misuse tools. The system prompt is therefore the “behavioral contract” that aligns the model with the harness’s expectations.An an example here's a system prompt for opencode ( opencode has it's own , so this isn't needed)You are an OpenCode assistant. Your only job is to produce **correct, runnable, minimal code** for the user. Always follow these rules: • Output **only code** unless the user explicitly asks for explanation. • Never invent APIs, functions, or libraries that do not exist. • Prefer standard libraries unless the user specifies otherwise. • If the request is ambiguous or impossible, state the limitation clearly. • Maintain deterministic formatting: no extra commentary, no mixed prose. • When multiple solutions exist, choose the simplest correct one. • If the user provides a codebase, follow its style and conventions exactly. • Do not modify unrelated parts of the code unless asked. • If you are unsure, ask for clarification instead of guessing.
item.guid: AI/Organized info/2 System Prompts and Tools/System Prompt for Harness.md

item.title: Memory Usage
item.description:
When you run a local model, your VRAM (or RAM, if offloading to CPU) gets split across a few distinct things, and it's worth knowing where it's all going: Model weights : the biggest fixed cost, determined by parameter count and Model Quantization level
Context & Kv Cache : grows with context length, separate from the weights Compute buffer / activations : temporary working memory the model needs during the actual forward pass, usually a smaller slice but not negligible
For example if you are planning to use a Dense model with 23Gb VRAM ( 24GB Vram but Os is using 1Gb ) and the model itself is 18Gb , this means you have 5Gb Extra VRAM for Context & Kv Cache. I would use 4Gb for Context in this scenario , so it has enough for compute buffer.
If your planning to use a MoE model then offloading isn't as bad , so your choosing what number of "experts" are loaded into VRAM , and what is offloaded.
The model total weight is "split" between VRAM+RAM , Kv Cache can be split ( but I recommend to keep it in VRAM for performance ).

item.title: KV cache Quantization
item.description: KV cache can be quantized , uses less memory for context , usually you start noticing on "higher" levels of context.K "is more important" than VIf a model gets lost in stuff @ 150k context with F16 , making the KV cache Q8 I would guess it would start to get "confused" at 120-130ishIn llama you use --cache-type-k / --cache-type-v. Requires -fa on (flash attention) for turbo types, or you eat a dequant penalty every step.Vector-quantized via "WHT + Lloyd-Max codebooks", calibration-free (no imatrix-style pre-pass needed). Numbers below are from real fork benchmarks (Qwen3-14B Q4_K_M, RX 9070 XT):Asymmetric best practice (per fork docs): K tolerates compression far worse than V. Something like ... --cache-type-k q8_0 --cache-type-v turbo4 rather than compressing both sides equallyRight now as I type this , turboquant is quite new.Turbo seems quite decent, but personally I woudn't go bellow Q8.

item.title: Model Quantization
item.description: I think most models start as 16-bit floats (BF16/FP16)every single "token" uses 4 bytes , by quantizing (simplifying here) we're making them use 8,6,4,2 bits.The model becomes smaller , but obviously with lossesSizes/RAM include ~10–15% compute buffer overhead. Does not include KV cache — size that separately based on context length and --cache-type-k/v.using Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (~36B params)Controls how many tensors get bumped to a higher bit-width than the base quant level — not a different algorithm. S (small): base bit-width almost everywhere — smallest file, most compression M (medium): a few sensitive tensors (some attention/output layers) raised one level — modest size increase, real accuracy gain L (large): more tensors raised — biggest of the three, closest to the tier above, sometimes overlaps it in practice Only Q2_K, Q3_K, Q4_K, Q5_K get S/M/L variants in stock llama.cpp. Q6_K and Q8_0 ship as single fixed formats with no split.At a size similar to Q4_K_S, it tends to preserve accuracy slightly better and decodes faster on CPU There is also many specific ways to "quantize" that aren't mentioned here, and probably many more to come. Quality-loss column is general guess Seems on Q8 it doesn't help , but Q6 and bellow it really helps.Afaik it's simillar to APEX , in training you check what weights in the model actually move the output, and give more "value" to those.APEX = "Adaptive Precision for EXpert Models" , it's specifically made for MoE models , since it uses different quants to different experts , it's nomenclature changes There are other things like this.

item.title: Moe Vs Dense
item.description: Right now models are mostly either "DENSE" or "MoE" ( Mixture of Experts)A Dense model will always activate everything , meaning if it's divided into several gpus or gpu+RAM , you will struggle more. Dense models are usually better , at least at the time of writing this a 30B Dense of same family will beat a 35B MoE.Dense models are more predictable since the model activates every token for calculations. At least for me , it's easier to finetune these models.If the model fits all in VRAM , use this.MoE models use experts , meaning when you call it , it will get only the experts, meaning obviously having the model in gpu+cpu isn't has "damaging" for the speed.It has a "routing" part ( that routes to experts ) that from my experience very hard to finetune and often is easy to break ( undoubtedly due to my lack of knowledge )It has the Experts , that are easier to finetune.Due to it's nature , it endsup being faster than Dense.If you wanna run a model that uses VRAM+RAM use this.
item.guid: AI/Organized info/3 Models Info/Moe Vs Dense.md
